title: Serverless Pay‑Per‑Use Compute
description: Eliminate idle infrastructure cost by running compute on a pay-per-execution billing model rather than paying for always-on instances. Well-suited to bursty or unpredictable workloads where provisioned servers would sit idle most of the time. This is a cost optimisation pattern, not an architectural style — it can apply to any workload that tolerates cold starts.

cost.costNotes: Estimates are intentionally conservative; verify against current pricing before committing. Free tiers are quota-limited; production use may require paid plans and compliance agreements.

provenance.estimatedMonthlyRangeUsd: Serverless compute platforms offer generous free tiers (AWS Lambda: 1M requests/month free, Azure Functions: 1M executions free, GCP Cloud Functions: 2M invocations free). Min=$0 represents low-traffic scenarios staying within free tier limits (e.g., development environments, webhooks, scheduled jobs with <100K invocations/month). Max=$500 represents moderate production workloads with ~10-20M invocations/month at $0.20 per million invocations plus compute time ($0.0000166667 per GB-second for AWS Lambda). Higher-scale workloads (>50M invocations) can exceed $1000-5000/month, but typical small-to-medium implementations fall in this range. Costs scale linearly with usage (requests × duration × memory), making serverless cost-effective for variable workloads but potentially expensive for constant high-throughput scenarios where traditional compute may be cheaper.
provenance.adoptionCost: Medium-high complexity adoption. Requires: (1) Architectural refactoring to stateless, event-driven functions (10-20 hours at $100-150/hour = $1000-3000), (2) Learning serverless-specific patterns (cold starts, timeouts, concurrency limits), (3) Rewriting deployment pipelines for function packaging and IAM configuration (5-10 hours = $500-1500), (4) Integration with cloud-native services (API Gateway, event buses, storage triggers). With AI coding assistants reducing boilerplate by 30-40%, estimate drops to ~$1500. Teams familiar with cloud-native development may spend $500-800; teams migrating from traditional monoliths may spend $2500-4000. Includes testing for cold start optimization and monitoring setup.
provenance.licenseCost: All major serverless platforms (AWS Lambda, Azure Functions, GCP Cloud Functions, Cloudflare Workers) operate on pure pay-per-use pricing with no upfront license fees or subscription requirements. No proprietary tools are mandatory—open-source frameworks (Serverless Framework, AWS SAM, Terraform) handle deployments. Optional paid tools like Datadog Serverless ($15/host-month) or Lumigo ($0.20/GB analyzed) exist for enhanced monitoring but aren't required. Enterprise support agreements (AWS Enterprise Support at 10% of monthly spend) are optional. Core platform access is free; customers pay only for compute resources consumed.

provides.reasoning: Serverless platforms automatically scale compute resources up and down based on demand without manual intervention, which is a core feature of this pattern.
Pay-per-use serverless compute eliminates costs during idle periods since you only pay for actual execution time, not for provisioned capacity.
This pattern provides compute execution capability, though in a serverless model, enabling workload processing without infrastructure management.
Serverless automatically provides elastic capacity to handle variable workloads without pre-provisioning, which is distinct from but related to auto-scaling.
The billing model charges only for actual function executions and duration, providing granular cost control based on usage.
Serverless abstracts away infrastructure management including servers, OS patching, and capacity planning, allowing focus on application code.

requires.reasoning: Serverless functions typically require stateless design patterns since execution environments are ephemeral and may not persist between invocations.
Serverless compute is typically triggered by events (HTTP requests, queue messages, scheduled events), requiring an event-driven architectural approach.
Serverless patterns must handle initialization latency when functions haven't been invoked recently, as cold starts are inherent to the platform's cost-saving mechanisms.

supports_nfr.reason: Serverless platforms typically provide 99.9-99.99% availability with automatic failover and regional redundancy.
Cold starts introduce 50-3000ms latency at p95 depending on runtime and package size.
Cold starts and occasional platform throttling cause p99 latency of 100-5000ms.
Serverless platforms automatically detect and route around failures within 1-2 minutes.
With quota increases, serverless can scale to 10,000+ concurrent executions achieving up to 36M jobs/hour.
Serverless functions can handle up to 50K read QPS with appropriate concurrency limits and auto-scaling.
Serverless write throughput is bounded by execution concurrency and downstream service limits at ~20K QPS.
Serverless platforms offer GDPR-compliant regions and data residency controls but pattern works regardless.
Major serverless providers offer HIPAA-eligible services with BAAs but pattern works for both compliant and non-compliant workloads.
Serverless compute supports CCPA compliance through data access controls and deletion capabilities but is not restricted to it.
Serverless platforms provide audit trails and controls for SOX compliance but pattern applies to all workload types.

supports_constraints.reason: The generic serverless pay-per-use cost pattern applies to cloud-agnostic specifications across AWS Lambda, Azure Functions, and GCP Cloud Functions.
Serverless platforms support major programming languages with managed runtimes and custom runtime support.
Serverless compute is ideal for backend workloads including APIs, workers, batch jobs, data pipelines, and ML inference.
Serverless functions can integrate with caching layers (Redis, ElastiCache) but pattern does not require caching.
Serverless functions work well with async messaging (SQS, EventBridge, Pub/Sub) but also support synchronous invocation.
Serverless compute supports AI inference workloads with appropriate memory and timeout configurations.
Serverless functions provide execution isolation suitable for multi-tenant architectures but work for single-tenant as well.
Serverless supports batch processing for jobs under execution time limits but also handles real-time request-response.
Distributed transactions are challenging in stateless serverless but can be implemented via saga patterns or not required.
Serverless functions can process real-time streams (Kinesis, Event Hubs) but also support batch and API workloads.
Serverless functions can integrate with vector databases for AI/ML applications but pattern does not mandate it.
Serverless functions commonly integrate with document stores (DynamoDB, Cosmos DB) but support any storage backend.
Serverless functions integrate well with key-value stores for fast lookups but pattern is storage-agnostic.
Serverless functions can query graph databases but pattern does not require or exclude graph data models.
Serverless functions can ingest and query time-series data but pattern is neutral to time-series requirements.
Serverless compute handles OLTP workloads with low-latency transactional processing but also supports analytical workloads.
Serverless functions can run OLAP queries and analytics jobs within execution time limits but pattern is workload-agnostic.

compute_type.description: Function (AWS Lambda, Cloud Functions) for sub-second tasks with faster scaling, or Container (Cloud Run, Fargate) for longer workloads with more control and fewer runtime constraints

memory_size_mb.description: Memory allocation directly impacts CPU allocation and cost. Higher memory = faster execution but higher per-invocation cost. Optimize based on workload profiling

timeout_seconds.description: Maximum execution time per invocation. Shorter timeouts prevent runaway costs but may fail legitimate long operations. Consider async patterns for long tasks

cold_start_optimization.description: Standard (pure pay-per-use, cold starts possible), Provisioned (pre-warmed instances, eliminates cold starts but adds baseline cost), Warm-pool (periodic warming, balanced approach)

concurrency_model.description: Auto (unlimited scaling, risk of cost spikes), Reserved (guaranteed capacity for critical workloads), Limited (cap concurrent executions to control costs and downstream load)

warn_nfr.reason: At very low job volumes (<100/hour), serverless invocation overhead and cold starts may outweigh the benefits of pay-per-use pricing compared to a simple always-on instance.
warn_nfr.message: arch-serverless-pay-per-use: peak jobs per hour is {actual} (<100). Serverless overhead may outweigh pay-per-use savings at this low volume.

warn_nfr.reason: At very low QPS (<1 req/s), serverless cold starts add disproportionate overhead; a simple always-on compute instance is more cost-effective.
warn_nfr.message: arch-serverless-pay-per-use: peak read QPS is {actual} req/s (<1). At this low traffic, cold start overhead makes serverless less cost-effective than a small always-on instance.

warn_nfr.reason: At sustained high QPS (>10K req/s), serverless pay-per-execution costs may exceed reserved capacity; analyze cost vs. performance trade-offs.
warn_nfr.message: arch-serverless-pay-per-use: peak read QPS is {actual} req/s (>10K). At sustained high throughput, pay-per-execution costs may exceed reserved compute instances. Evaluate cost model.
